content.assessment: This proposal leaves tracking age untouched, which is the central variable the Finnish comprehensive lens identifies as consequential; from this lens, admissions-mechanism reform alone addresses a different lever than the one Finland changed. The lens cannot directly endorse or refute this approach, since it operates on selection method rather than selection age, but Finland's post-1990s experience is relevant: within-school and choice-based sorting can reproduce segregation even absent formal early tracking, suggesting admissions redesign without tracking-age change risks being an incomplete fix analogous to Finland's post-1990s partial erosion.
opportunities: Could be paired with later comprehensive extension, using the Finnish sequence of procedural reform preceding structural reform as a possible template, though this is speculative beyond the lens evidence provided
strengths: Administrative desegregation precedent showing admissions-level intervention can shift outcomes, which is structurally analogous to how Finland's within-school selection mechanisms interact with formal tracking status
threats: Finland's post-1990s experience demonstrates that fine-grained selection mechanisms (within-school class selection) can reintroduce segregation even without formal early tracking, suggesting admissions-only reform in Hungary may face a similar ceiling on effectiveness while leaving the higher-order tracking-age driver unaddressed
weaknesses: Does not engage the tracking-age variable that the Finnish comprehensive-school experience centers on, limiting the lens's ability to assess core mechanism alignment
